vitals.location: Punat, Island of Krk, Primorje-Gorski Kotar County, Croatia
vitals.field_size: 400 starters (2026, ~500-rider advertised cap sold out ~6 weeks prior)
vitals.cutoff_time: Relative cutoff at km-52 control: riders 40+ min behind the leader diverted to a shortened finish

climate.description: Late-April Adriatic island weather — no organizer-published weather or wind data for this specific route.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: original score not on file. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 54. EDITORIAL WAVE 10 (2026-07-24): elevation added (1,320m, was null) and distance/field confirmed via official race propositions — dimension scores below were NOT re-evaluated against this newly-confirmed profile (a real 1,320m/82km rolling island course with a punishing relative cutoff is more demanding than a 'null elevation' placeholder implies); flagging for human rescoring rather than silently changing numbers.

course_description.character: An island-wide paved road circuit out of Punat on Krk, one of Croatia's largest Adriatic islands (second-largest after Cres). The 82km route runs north from Punat through Krk town, Malinska, Njivice and Omišalj, cuts inland through the karst villages of Dobrinj and Vrbnik, climbs to Treskavac Pass, descends into Baška on the island's south coast, then turns back over a 7.9km climb toward Punat. Four categorized ascents break up otherwise rolling terrain — nothing alpine, but persistent enough that the course rewards pacing over raw power.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Vrbnik-side climb to Treskavac Pass, immediately followed by the long descent into Baška.
suffering_zones: Km 52.2: 3.1km at 5.8% (Category 3), also the relative-cutoff control point
Km 67.0: 7.9km at 3.6% (Category 3) — the return climb toward Punat

history.origin_story: Inaugural edition held in Punat on April 27, 2024, organized by Hoteli Punat/Falkensteiner Punat with support from the Punat Tourist Board; Marin Kirin and Toni Dujmenović have directed the race since. Run with the Croatian Cycling Federation and Granfondo World Tour, though it is not a UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier.
history.reputation: A young but quickly-sold-out event — the 2026 field cap was reached about six weeks before race day.

biased_opinion.verdict: Real, Young, Popular — But Read the Fine Print
biased_opinion.summary: Krk Gran Fondo is only in its third edition in 2026. Sources variously report 400 actual starters, roughly 450 expected riders, and a 500-place registration allocation — organizer materials aren't internally consistent on this point, but registration was reported sold out about six weeks ahead, and a 600-rider allocation is already open for 2027. The course is a rolling-island circuit with four categorized climbs and a Treskavac-to-Baška high point, not a flat coastal cruise. The organizer's paperwork has real problems: the 2026 race propositions PDF misprints the year, inconsistently rounds the distance, and carries over a stray reference to package pickup in Zadar — worth double-checking any detail against the live event page rather than the PDF alone. The relative cutoff at km 52 (riders 40+ minutes behind the leader are diverted to a shortened finish) is also stricter than the 'fondo' framing suggests for a recreational field.
strengths: Registration reportedly sold out ~6 weeks ahead in 2026, with a 600-rider allocation already open for 2027
Rolling-island course with four categorized climbs and a Treskavac Pass high point
Post-race coverage reports extensive road control and a 190-person event operating team
Adriatic island route through Malinska, Njivice, Omišalj, Dobrinj, Vrbnik and Baška
weaknesses: Official race-propositions PDF has real document-control problems (wrong year printed, distance rounding inconsistencies, a leftover Zadar reference) — don't take the PDF as gospel over the live event page
Riders 40+ minutes behind the leader at the km-52 control are diverted to a shortened finish — stricter than typical recreational-fondo pacing
Not a UCI Gran Fondo World Series qualifier, unlike nearby Istria Gran Fondo — lower competitive prestige
Young event (3rd edition in 2026) with a correspondingly thin track record
Organizer's own field-size reporting is inconsistent (400 vs. ~450 vs. 500 across different materials)
biased_opinion.bottom_line: An island road race for riders who want real Adriatic climbing without epic length — verify logistics against the live site, and pace conservatively given the km-52 cutoff.

final_verdict.one_liner: A young Krk Island road race with four categorized climbs and a stricter-than-expected mid-race cutoff.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes, if you want a compact (82km) Adriatic island road race with real climbing and don't mind registering early — it reportedly sold out weeks ahead in 2026. Pace conservatively: riders 40+ minutes behind the leader at km 52 are diverted to a shortened finish.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a longer, UCI-qualifying Croatian gran fondo: Istria Gran Fondo (Umag, 105km, UCI Gran Fondo World Series). For another Adriatic island event: Granfondo Pag - Cycling on the Moon (Novalja, ~107km/1,210m).

catalog_flags.status_note: Two of three youtube_data videos removed as non-matching this wave (2026-07-24): 'Tour Of Krk' (2015, generic island cycling footage, not this race) and 'Krk wine & bike' (a private tour operator's ad, unrelated to the organized event). Kept: '2025 Krk Granfondo Falkensteiner' — matches the event by name, organizer (Falkensteiner Punat), and near-identical date to the confirmed 2026 edition.
